abstract:
This article provides a comprehensive analysis of the concluding pericope of the Book of Sirach (51:13–30) as preserved in the Greek tradition. The text is structured into two distinct movements: an autobiographical reflection (vv. 13–22) in which the author recounts his lifelong pursuit of Wisdom, followed by a didactic summons (vv. 23–30) inviting the unlearned to enter his school.

The Wisdom described here is fundamentally religious, rooted in prayer and expressed through moral action. The author argues that while the quest for Wisdom should ideally begin in youth, it requires a holistic commitment, demanding that the seeker subordinate all aspects of life to this pursuit. The pericope delineates the essential path to Wisdom, which includes purification, adherence to the Law, and a steadfast commitment to righteousness. In return, the seeker receives "good possessions"—notably joy, intellectual clarity, and a "tongue" capable of both praising God and instructing others.

A central theme of the text is the sage’s accessibility. Unlike the more pessimistic outlooks found in Job or Ecclesiastes, Sirach suggests that Wisdom is within reach and does not require Herculean effort, provided the search remains constant. The author presents two complementary paths to enlightenment: personal searching and learning from the established experience of a teacher. Ultimately, this pericope serves as a vital summation of Ben Sira’s theology. Functioning as a literary bookend alongside the prologue (1:1–10), it synthesizes the book’s major theological themes and underscores the author’s optimism regarding the human capacity to encounter the Divine.
